4 In one of the three rolls he included directions for his funeral; in the second, an account of what he had accomplished, which he desired to have cut upon bronze tablets and set up at the entrance to the Mausoleum;⁠ 162 in the third, a summary of the condition of the whole empire; how many soldiers there were in active service in all parts of it, how much money there was in the public treasury and in the privy-purse, and what revenues were in arrears. He added, besides, the names of the freedmen and slaves from whom the details could be demanded.
sections.la:
1 Testamentum L. Planco C. Silio cons. III. Non. Apriles, ante annum et quattuor menses quam decederet, factum ab eo ac duobus codicibus partim ipsius partim libertorum Polybi et Hilarionis manu scriptum depositumque apud se virgines Vestales cum tribus signatis aeque voluminibus protulerunt. Quae omnia in senatu aperta atque recitata sunt.

2 Heredes instituit primos: Tiberium ex parte dimidia et sextante, Liviam ex parte tertia, quos et ferre nomen suum iussit; secundos: Drusum Tiberi filium ex triente, ex partibus reliquis Germanicum liberosque eius tres sexus virilis; tertio gradu propinquos, amicosque compluris. Legavit populo R. quadringenties, tribubus tricies quinquies sestertium, praetorianis militibus singula milia nummorum, cohortibus urbanis quingenos, legionaris trecenos nummos; quam summam repraesentari iussit, nam et confiscatam semper repositamque habuerat.

3 Reliqua legata varie dedit perduxitque quaedam ad vicena sestertia, quibus solvendis annuum diem finiit, excusata rei familiaris mediocritate nec plus perventurum ad heredes suos quam milies et quingenties professus, quamvis viginti proximis annis quaterdecies milies ex testamentis amicorum percepisset, quod paene omne cum duobus paternis patrimoniis ceterisque hereditatibus in rem p. absumpsisset. Iulias filiam neptemque, si quid iis accidisset, vetuit sepulcro suo inferri.

4 Tribus voluminibus, uno mandata de funere suo complexus est, altero indicem rerum a se gestarum, quem vellet incidi in aeneis tabulis, quae ante Mausoleum statuerentur, tertio breviarium totius imperii, quantum militum sub signis ubique esset, quantum pecuniae in aerario et fiscis et vectigaliorum residuis. Adiecit et libertorum servorumque nomina, a quibus ratio exigi posset.

notes.96: The "five-day" festival of Minerva, March 20‑25. Thayer's Note: For comprehensive details and sources, see the article Quinquatrus in Smith's Dictionary of Greek and Roman Antiquities .
notes.97: Commonly called peperino , a hard grey volcanic stone with black nodules resembling peppercorns.
notes.99: With reference to the study of Archimedes, or perhaps to the general use of such elevated rooms in Syracuse.
notes.101: Opposed to vestis forensis or forensia ( vestimenta ); cf. Vit. viii.1 .
notes.102: The cena recta was a regular dinner, at which the guests reclined on couches at a table, contrasted with sportula , an informal meal ( Claud. xxi.4) or a distribution of food. See Friedländer, Roman Life and Manners (Eng. trans.), IV. pp77 ff.
notes.104: Tellers of marvellous tales; cf. Juv. 15.16 , and Mayor ad loc. Doubtless the same as the fabulatores , lxxviii.2 , below. Thayer's Note: the Greek word means "people who discourse on virtue". See the interesting entry Aretalogi in Smith's Dictionary of Greek and Roman Antiquities .
notes.105: See chap. xxxi.5 and note ; some think that the reference is to the • Regia in the Forum.
notes.106: That is, grapes suited for eating and not for making wine; cf. Mart. 13.22 ; Colum. 3.2 .
notes.108: That is, without a blanket over his feet, because he had his shoes on.
notes.111: Roman measure; • a little less than five feet seven inches (5·58) English.
notes.112: Ursa major, Charles's Wain, the Great Dipper.
notes.113: Apparently a form of poultice; some read habenarum and explain as a kind of truss. Thayer's Note: The reading habenarum makes good sense to me; see the use of habenae for treating fractures in Celsus, VIII .10 , and note (subsection C ) that such a treatment would stretch the bone, a desirable effect in the case of a limp.
notes.114: Cf. Hor. Epist. 1.15.2‑3 . Thayer's Note: For medical commentary and further context in ancient medicine, see Spencer's Introduction to Book V of Celsus .
notes.116: Albulae aquae were the sulphur springs which flow into the Anio between Rome and Tivoli ( Tibur ). Thayer's Note: For further sources, geographical indications, etc., see the article Albulae Aquae .
notes.117: The pila was a small hard ball. Three players stood at the three points of a triangle (whence the game was called trigon ) and passed the ball from one to the other. A skilful player used his left hand as well as his right. Thayer's Note: For comprehensive details and sources and a woodcut, see the article Pila in Smith's Dictionary of Greek and Roman Antiquities .
notes.118: The folliculus was a large light ball. The players wore a guard on the right arm, with which they struck the ball, as in the Italian gioco del pallone . Thayer's Note: For further sources, see the article Follis in Smith's Dictionary of Greek and Roman Antiquities .
notes.119: Many games were played with nuts; cf. Hor. Serm. 2.3.171 , Mart. 5.84 , etc.
notes.120: See chap. lxxxi at the end .
notes.121: Brutus published a eulogy of Cato in 46 B.C. ; cf. Cic. ad Att. 12.21 .
notes.122: Evidently two archaizing grammarians of the day.
notes.123: See De Grammaticis , x, at the end .
notes.124: Thus characterized in contrast with the studied simplicity of the Attic school of orators.
notes.126: Cf. Catull. 67.21 , languidior tenera beta . All these words, which Augustus is said to have used, are colloquialisms or [[PAGE (p255)]] slang, and the exact form and derivation of many of them are uncertain.
notes.127: Since sumus was originally enclitic, the forms simus, sumus may have represented the sound between u and i in maximus, maxumus , etc. Or simus may have been formed on the analogy of agimus and similar forms.
notes.128: Domuos is the earlier form of the genitive, with the suffix -os ; domus the later, with the suffix -s . There was no form domos , and if Augustus used it, he probably did so on the analogy of domos — domus in the acc. plur.
notes.129: Cf. Jul. lvi.6‑7 , and Aul. Gell. 17.9.1‑5 .
notes.130: Religiones includes both religious belief and regard for omens and portents.
notes.131: Plin. N. H. ii.55 , says that the laurel tree ( cf. Tib. lxix ) and the seal are never struck by lightning; and also that lightning never goes more than • five feet below the ground.
notes.133: This is not in accordance with the emperor's character ( cf. chap. lvii ), and Suetonius may have confused him with Caligula; see Calig. xlii . Yet there are records of many such acts of humility to propitiate Nemesis; see Casaubon ad loc.
notes.134: The Roman month was divided into periods of eight days, lettered in the Calendar from A to H . The last of these, every ninth day ( nundinae ) according to the Roman reckoning, was a market day. Thayer's Note: This is misleading, leaving the impression that the nundinal periods were subdivisions of the month. Like our own weeks, they were not. For comprehensive details and sources, see the article Nundinae in Smith's Dictionary of Greek and Roman Antiquities .
notes.135: Because of its resemblance to non is (from eo ); cf. Cic. de Div. 2.84 ; º or perhaps merely because it contained non .
notes.136: Into the Eleusinian mysteries of Ceres.
notes.137: The decree was not complete until this was done; cf. Jul. xxviii.3 .
notes.138: i.e. "Discourses about the Gods." Aristotle wrote a work with the same title.
notes.139: The genius , or familiar spirit (see note on chap. lx ), was [[PAGE (p265)]] often represented by a serpent, and those of husband and wife by two serpents; e.g. in Pompeian frescoes. Thayer's Note: In Roman art, as far as anyone can tell, serpents as genii appear to be a motif of Etruscan origin; at any rate, they appear very frequently in very old Etruscan tombs and sculpture. For a plethora of them, with various explanations, see this long passage of George Dennis's Cities and Cemeteries of Etruria , without neglecting the notes.
notes.140: To avoid profanation of the sacred rites.
notes.141: Otherwise unknown; Müller would read Caesarem Drusum . Stahr believes that the reference is to the Eulogy in chap. c.3 .
notes.142: Apparently another name for the via Appia; see CIL I .1291 . Thayer's Note: For a different and firmer opinion, see the article Via Campana in Platner and Ashby's Topographical Dictionary of Ancient Rome .
notes.145: Marked by the broad purple stripe ( latus clavus ). Augustus was not yet a senator, but the privilege of wearing the laticlave was doubtless one of the honours conferred on him by Caesar.
notes.148: The lustrum was a sacrifice of purification, made every five years by one of the censors, after the completion of the census, or enumeration of the Roman people. The sacrifice consisted of the suovetaurilia , the offering of a pig, a sheep, and a bull. Lustrum was also applied to the five-year period. Thayer's Note: For comprehensive details and sources, see the article Lustrum in Smith's Dictionary of Greek and Roman Antiquities .
notes.149: The pallium was the distinctive dress of the Greeks, as the toga was of the Romans.
notes.150: Greek youths between the ages of eighteen and that of full citizen ­ship, who had regular gymnastic training as a part of their education. The Greek training survived in Capreae, which until recently (see chap. xcii.2 ) had belonged to Naples.
notes.151: The City of Do-Nothings . There is no island "near Capreae," and "the neighbouring island of Capreae" is meaningless; if the text is sound, Suetonius is careless, or we must take Capreis as a locative, and regard vicinam as used in a partitive sense like reliquus , primus , etc.
notes.152: Κτίστης , the Greek name for the founder of a city or colony.
notes.157: Augustus and Augusta , but Tiberius did not assume the title until it was conferred on him by the senate; Dio 57.2‑3 .
notes.160: Probably those with which he was connected (see chap. xl.2 ); Lipsius suggested tribulibus .
notes.161: That is, on their death; a common euphemism.
notes.162: The original of this inscription is lost, but the greater part of a copy inscribed in Greek and Latin on marble is preserved at Ancyra in Asia Minor and is known as the Monumentum Ancyranum . Thayer's Notes: a The 23rd of September was Augustus's birthdate in the calendar of the time. It was, however, out of whack with the seasons, as is proved by the frequent statement in ancient authors that his birth sign was Capricorn; some modern writers state that Capricorn was the sign under which he was conceived. Other evidence as to the actual date of his birthday includes the location of the Ara Pacis in relation to the Obelisk of Augustus (see this article) and some of the emperor's coinage, depicting Capricorn, said to be the sign under which he was born (or, according to others — seeking to get around the calendrical difficulties, I think — conceived). A page on this whole question may eventually show up on this site in the fullness of time; this is one of the pages slated to receive a link to it when it does.
notes.b: An excellent paper on Augustus's Nicopolis campsite memorial was once available online at Harvard; with the continued shrinkage of the Web, it has vanished.
notes.c: This note received from Prof. John D. Morgan at the University of Delaware: There's a foolish error in the Loeb translation of Suetonius, D. Aug. 19.1: also Asinius Epicadus, a half-breed of Parthian descent, . . . The corresponding Latin is item Asini Epicadi ex gente Parthina hybridae, . . . ' Parthina ' means not 'Parthian' but 'of the Parthini', the Illyrian tribe over which Asinius Pollio had celebrated his triumph in 39 B.C. This is a bad enough mistake that you might want to violate your usual policy of not correcting errors in your sources. This mistake is probably not one I would have caught on my own — and when putting up translations I don't check them systematically against the original — but Loeb translations are often not the best and do fall into just this kind of foolish error, and the Lewis & Short so defines Parthinus (although it too, like all of us, falls into the occasional error) so I've gratefully incorporated Dr. Morgan's correction.
notes.d: It wasn't much of a confusion, nor consequently of a restoration. There had been a communication problem, a translation error between the Greek astronomer who had done the calculations, and the bureaucrats who implemented it: "every fourth year" for the leap years had been understood by the Latin-speakers as meaning "every third year", since Latin counts inclusively but Greek, as we do today, exclusively. If continued, this would have put the calendar off by a cumulative systematic error of one day every twelve years. Augustus ordered that the intercalation be set right to one day every fourth year, and that some leap years be skipped to make up for the extra days since 45 B.C. Historians looking for exact dates in Augustus' reign thus need to take into account this slippage and its correction; their task is complicated by our not knowing exactly when the error was caught: see Chris Bennett's very probable solution in his long, thorough note (on an excellent page about Cleopatra, by the way).
notes.e: Crucifixions, which appear to have been quite frequent, were carried out along the public roads into the city; the connection, very likely, is not so much one of participating in the prince's mercy, but a cosmetic desire that his eyes should not see these brutal executions. (I cannot avoid being reminded of the firm custom, in the area around Assisi, of not hunting on the feast day of St. Francis.)
notes.f: That is, games held every four years. ( Not every five; if this puzzles you, see the article Quinquennalia in Smith's Dictionary of Greek and Roman Antiquities , and my note there.)
notes.g: A bit of Loeb prudery: the Latin text reads intactam adhuc et virginem , "untouched until then, and still a virgin."
notes.h: An elegant sea resort in Roman times, mentioned also in Suet. Tib. 72.2 , and in Plut. Cic. 47 as the site of a villa of Cicero's to which he fled in his last days and near which he was killed. It was heavily fortified in the 12c and is now the small town of Torre Astura in Latina province: it is about 15 km SE of Anzio ( Antium ) by road. See for example this photo site .
